name: Apollo's Bow
description: Apollo's Bow, a legendary weapon in Greek mythology, is far more than a simple hunting tool or instrument of war. Forged by Hephaestus, the god of the forge, from silver and gold, it embodies the power and precision of its divine owner. The bow is intrinsically linked to Apollo's multifaceted nature, representing not only his skill in archery but also his association with healing, plague, and music. Its arrows are unerring, capable of delivering swift justice or spreading disease with equal ease. The bow's golden gleam symbolizes Apollo's connection to the sun and light, while its deadly accuracy reflects his capacity for both creation and destruction. It is a symbol of divine authority, a tool wielded by a god who embodies both the beauty and the terror of the natural world. The bow is often depicted as being strung with the sinews of mythical beasts, further emphasizing its divine and potent nature. Its arrows, when loosed, are said to sing with a deadly harmony, a testament to Apollo's mastery of both music and archery. To possess or wield Apollo's Bow would be to command a power far beyond mortal comprehension, a power capable of shaping destinies and altering the course of history.
subtitle: A bow of divine power wielded by Apollo.
currentLocation: Lost to the ages; whereabouts unknown.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Python
keyMyths.description: Apollo used his bow and arrows to slay Python, the serpent guarding Delphi. This act established Apollo's control over the Delphic oracle and cemented his reputation as a powerful god capable of overcoming monstrous foes. The arrows flew true, piercing Python's scales and bringing about its demise, a testament to the bow's accuracy and power.

keyMyths.title: The Trojan War Plagues
keyMyths.description: During the Trojan War, Apollo unleashed a plague upon the Achaean army, punishing Agamemnon for his disrespect towards Chryses, a priest of Apollo. The arrows of Apollo's Bow rained down upon the Greek camp, carrying disease and death, highlighting the bow's capacity for destruction and divine retribution. This plague was a pivotal event influencing the direction of the war.

keyMyths.title: The Niobe Massacre
keyMyths.description: Niobe, Queen of Thebes, boasted that she was superior to Leto because she had more children. In retaliation, Apollo and Artemis, Leto's children, used their bows to kill Niobe's sons and daughters. Apollo's arrows, launched with deadly precision, struck down the male offspring, demonstrating the swift and merciless justice of the gods.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides accounts of Apollo's intervention in the Trojan War, including the use of his bow to spread plague.
sources.text: Provides accounts of Apollo's intervention in the Trojan War, including the use of his bow to spread plague.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: Details the story of Niobe and the devastating consequences of her hubris, showcasing Apollo's bow's power.
sources.text: Details the story of Niobe and the devastating consequences of her hubris, showcasing Apollo's bow's power.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: References Apollo's divine lineage and attributes, indirectly alluding to the power associated with his possessions, including his bow.
